reasons.code_quality: The submitted application files are cleanly structured and idiomatic FastAPI/SQLAlchemy code, but this is entirely irrelevant to the task at hand.
reasons.completeness: The one required deliverable — updating the cryptography version in requirements.lock — is completely absent; no lock file was submitted at all.
reasons.edge_cases: No task-relevant work was performed, so there are no edge cases to evaluate for the actual lock-file edit.
reasons.security: settings.py contains a hardcoded JWT secret ('dev-secret-do-not-use-in-prod') in source code; the comment acknowledges it but it is still a committed credential, and the submission ignores the actual security task (patching a vulnerable cryptography version).
reasons.plan_adherence: A correct plan would produce exactly one changed line in requirements.lock; instead the submission delivers a full application codebase with no trace of the required change.
reasons.scope_creep: The submission includes the entire application source tree (database.py, main.py, models, routes, schemas, settings, tests, pyproject.toml, README) — none of which are within scope for a single dependency bump in a lock file.
reasons.over_engineering: The task required a one-line edit; the submission delivered a complete multi-file application scaffold, which is the most extreme possible over-delivery relative to the task scope.

overall_notes: The submission completely misses the task: requirements.lock was never touched, and the cryptography version was never bumped. Instead, what appears to be the baseline seed-project codebase was submitted wholesale, suggesting the wrong files were attached or the task was misread entirely. Every dimension suffers as a result — the only thing that scores above 1 is code quality, and only because the app code itself is structurally sound despite being irrelevant.
